meta.description: Weights for how each domain impacts household stability and priority flows. All weights are normalized to 0.0–1.0. Higher = stronger impact or bias.
meta.intendedUse: Scheduling, Planning Graph next-step biasing, and SessionRunner 'Now' CTA prioritization.

storehouse.description: Core food and supplies backbone. When the storehouse is stable, all other domains become easier to run smoothly.

cooking.description: High-frequency, time-sensitive domain that directly affects daily comfort and nutrition. Strong driver of short-term stability.

cleaning.description: Environmental quality, hygiene, and clarity. Important for mental and physical stability but slightly less time-critical than cooking.

garden.description: Seasonal and weather-driven. Very time-sensitive in windows, but impacts medium-to-long-term stability rather than daily operations.

animals.description: High responsibility and life-safety impact. Missed tasks can have serious consequences, so this domain gets strong priority during active windows.

preservation.description: Locks in the value of harvests, batches, and butchery. Key for long-term stability, especially when there is fresh surplus.

flowMatrix.reason: Storehouse stock directly drives cooking sessions and meal planning.
Detected surplus or looming expiry in storehouse should nudge preservation sessions.
Bulk restocking and storage changes often require cleaning or reorganization.
Cooking sessions naturally generate cleaning tasks; cleaning should frequently be the next step.
Cooking depletes inventory and should trigger storehouse adjustments or audits.
Batch cooking often leads to leftovers better preserved for later use.
Deep cleaning of zones often exposes forgotten items or reorganized storage that should be reflected in the storehouse.
Cleaned kitchen or prep areas can encourage immediate cooking or batch sessions.
Harvest events must be reflected in storehouse inventory and savings.
Perishable harvests strongly drive preservation sessions like canning, freezing, or dehydrating.
Fresh harvests increase cooking opportunities and recipe suggestions.
Butchery and egg/milk collection significantly change storehouse protein and fat stocks.
Meat processing and preserving are time-sensitive after butchery tasks.
Animal care and butchery generate immediate cleaning and sanitizing requirements.
Preservation sessions culminate in updated storehouse counts and long-term inventory stability.
Newly preserved items should be surfaced in future cooking and meal planning suggestions.
Canning and preserving leave equipment and zones needing cleaning and reset.
Storehouse gaps and projected shortages should drive garden planting plans.
Protein and fat gaps can influence decisions about animal stocking and breeding.
